name: Kilbourne Hole
short_description: BLM rockhounding site near Las Cruces featuring peridot and olivine crystals from a maar volcanic crater. Free public access with a 25 lb daily limit.
description:
Kilbourne Hole is a maar - a volcanic crater formed by explosive interaction between magma and groundwater - located on flat BLM land in southern Doña Ana County. This geologically unique site exposes olivine-rich basalt ejecta that contains gem-quality peridot crystals and olivine nodules. The crater itself is a spectacular landmark rising above the surrounding desert floor, with extensive surrounding areas open for recreational rockhounding.

The site is particularly valued by rockhounds for its peridot specimens, which range from small facet-quality stones to larger crystal groups. Olivine is the primary mineral in the ejecta blanket surrounding the crater, and weathered specimens often reveal gem-quality material. The flat terrain and straightforward access make this a popular destination for both beginners and experienced collectors.

Kilbourne Hole sits at 3,923 feet elevation in the Chihuahuan Desert, where Quaternary basaltic volcanism created this dramatic formation. The sparse vegetation typical of high desert makes the exposed geology highly visible. No permits are required for personal use collecting, and BLM regulations allow 25 pounds per day per person.

The area offers excellent geology and clear skies, making it both a collecting site and an educational location. Views of surrounding desert mountains and the stark volcanic landscape make a visit here memorable. Early morning or late afternoon visits avoid peak heat and provide better light for spotting crystals in the ejecta.
history: Kilbourne Hole formed approximately 130,000 years ago as a maars volcano when rising basaltic magma encountered groundwater, causing phreatomagmatic eruptions. The explosive interactions ejected olivine-rich basalt fragments and xenoliths across the surrounding desert, creating the peridot-bearing deposits that rockhounds seek today. The crater walls and surrounding ejecta blanket expose cross-sections of the Quaternary basaltic volcanism that characterizes the Rio Grande Rift zone in southern New Mexico.
tips: Search the dark ejecta blanket surrounding the crater base, where olivine-rich basalt fragments concentrate in lag deposits after wind and water erosion. Use a hand lens to inspect broken basalt pieces for translucent green peridot or olive-toned olivine crystals catching the light. Focus on areas where the ground surface is littered with volcanic rocks rather than fine sand, as these stone concentrations are where weathered specimens accumulate. Bring a geology hammer or rock chisel to crack open fresh samples and expose interior gem-quality material.
terrain_notes: Elevation 3,923 feet on flat to gently undulating high desert terrain with scattered low shrubs and creosote. The surrounding ejecta field spreads across hardpan and sandy desert floor with excellent visibility. Walking distances from parking to prime collecting areas are typically short, under 0.5 miles. Footing is stable on packed desert surface with scattered small rocks; no major obstacles or steep slopes.
best_season: October - April. High desert heat becomes extreme May through September, with temperatures often exceeding 100 degrees F. Winter months offer comfortable 50-70 degree temperatures ideal for extended collecting sessions. Desert storms are rare but possible in monsoon season July-September.
written_directions: From Las Cruces, take US 70 east toward El Paso for approximately 30 miles. Continue on US 70 east past Radium Springs. Turn south on County Road A113 (signed for Kilbourne Hole) and follow approximately 5 miles to the BLM parking area and crater overlook. The road is paved initially, becoming improved dirt. High-clearance passenger vehicles can access the site, though some washboard is present on the final approach.
road_conditions: Paved on US 70 and initial county road sections. Final 2-3 miles are improved dirt with light washboard. Passenger vehicles suitable.
parking_notes: BLM parking area and crater overlook with multiple informal pullouts. Capacity for approximately 10-15 vehicles. No facilities present.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA guidelines. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Respect the volcanic crater - do not climb crater walls or remove large samples. Collect from ejecta field only.
rules:

nearest_services: Las Cruces, approximately 30 miles west. Full services including gas, restaurants, lodging, and water. Radium Springs, approximately 20 miles west, has limited services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat, stable terrain accessible to most hikers and visitors with normal mobility. Parking area has close access to collecting sites. Improved dirt road is passable for standard vehicles. No wheelchair accessibility, but collecting areas do not require hiking or climbing. Sturdy walking shoes recommended for navigating rocky ejecta field.

faq.question: What gems can you find at Kilbourne Hole near Las Cruces?
faq.answer: Kilbourne Hole is best known for peridot and olivine crystals found in the dark basaltic ejecta blanket surrounding the maar crater. Peridot specimens range from small facet-quality stones to larger crystal clusters. You may also find garnets and other olivine-rich basalt fragments. Most collectors focus on the ejecta field where weathering has exposed gem-quality material.

faq.question: Is there a permit or fee required to rockhound at Kilbourne Hole?
faq.answer: No permit or fee is required at Kilbourne Hole - it is free public BLM land open to recreational rockhounding. You do not need to register or contact any agency. Simply follow BLM collection rules: 25 pounds per day, personal use only, no motorized excavation.

faq.question: How far is Kilbourne Hole from Las Cruces and what is the drive like?
faq.answer: Kilbourne Hole is approximately 30 miles east of Las Cruces via US 70. The drive takes about 45 minutes. Most of the route is paved highway. The final 2-3 miles become improved dirt road with light washboard, easily passable by standard passenger vehicles.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Kilbourne Hole?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal, when high desert temperatures range from 50-70 degrees F. Summer months May-September bring extreme heat exceeding 100 degrees F. Winter months offer comfortable conditions and minimal rain risk. Early morning or late afternoon visits maximize comfort and provide better light for spotting peridot crystals.

faq.question: Can families and kids rockhound at Kilbourne Hole?
faq.answer: Yes, Kilbourne Hole is family-friendly for ages 6 and up. The flat terrain requires no hiking, and the volcanic crater offers educational geology interest. Kids enjoy the dramatic landscape and searching for peridot crystals in the ejecta field. Keep children away from crater walls and bring plenty of water, sun protection, and light snacks.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need for rockhounding peridot at Kilbourne Hole?
faq.answer: Bring a geology hammer or rock chisel to crack open basalt pieces and expose interior peridot crystals. A hand lens helps identify translucent green gems in broken samples. Wear sturdy shoes for walking across rocky terrain. Essential items: sunscreen, wide-brimmed hat, plenty of water, field bag for samples. A collecting license is not required on BLM land.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding for peridot and olivine at Kilbourne Hole near Las Cruces, NM. Maar volcanic crater, no permit needed, 25 lb daily limit.
